text.title: Prometheus Core Concepts
text.summary: Metric types, naming conventions, and label cardinality rules — the foundation of a maintainable Prometheus setup.

section.title: Metric Types
section.p: Prometheus has four metric types. Choose based on what the value represents, not how you want to display it.

list.item: Counter: monotonically increasing value; never decreases except on process restart. Use for request count, errors, bytes sent. Always suffix with _total.
Gauge: value that can go up and down. Use for queue size, active connections, temperature, memory usage.
Histogram: samples observations into configurable buckets. Use for request duration and response sizes. Exposes _bucket, _sum, _count series. Required for histogram_quantile().
Summary: client-side quantile calculation over a sliding window. Prefer Histogram — Summaries cannot be aggregated across instances.

rule.statement: Prefer Histogram over Summary for latency metrics in distributed systems.
rule.rationale: Histograms are aggregatable across replicas with sum(rate(...)) by (le). Summary quantiles are calculated per-instance and cannot be meaningfully combined across multiple pods.

rule.statement: Follow the pattern: namespace_name_unit_suffix. Use snake_case. Suffix counters with _total; histograms with _seconds or _bytes.
rule.rationale: Consistent naming allows tooling (Grafana, alerting) to auto-discover metrics and enables teams to understand a metric's purpose without documentation.

example.code: http_requests_total # Counter http_request_duration_seconds_bucket # Histogram bucket db_connections_active # Gauge process_memory_bytes # Gauge job:http_requests:rate5m # Recording rule (level:metric:operation)
httpRequests # camelCase, no unit, no suffix request_count_total # 'count' is redundant; just _total latency # No unit, no namespace

rule.statement: Never use unbounded values as labels: no user IDs, request IDs, email addresses, or UUIDs. Each unique label combination creates a new time series.
rule.rationale: High cardinality causes Prometheus OOM crashes. 1M users × 10 endpoints = 10M time series from a single metric. Labels must have bounded value sets: HTTP methods, status code classes, service names.

rule.statement: Keep label count per metric at or below 10. Add only labels needed for meaningful aggregation or filtering in dashboards and alerts.
rule.rationale: Each additional label multiplies the number of time series exponentially. Labels that are never used in queries waste memory and slow down scrapes.

example.code: # Good: bounded labels (method has ~5 values, status ~10, endpoint ~50) REQUEST_COUNT = Counter( 'http_requests_total', 'Total HTTP requests', ['method', 'endpoint', 'status'] )
# Bad: unbounded labels — user_id creates millions of time series REQUEST_COUNT = Counter( 'http_requests_total', 'Total HTTP requests', ['method', 'user_id', 'request_id'] # Never do this )
